svg.text: THE GAME OF LOGIC
Instructions
What You Need
· 9 counters (4 red, 5 grey)
· cardboard diagram board (smaller 2x2 grid and larger diagram for 3 attributes)
· instruction book
How to Play
Lewis Carroll's logic game uses a small cardboard diagram (a grid of
compartments) and nine counters: four red and five grey. Red counters mean
'at least one thing exists here,' grey counters mean 'nothing exists here.'
To solve a logical puzzle (called a syllogism), you mark the premises on the
large diagram using counters, then transfer the result to the smaller diagram
to read the conclusion. One player can work through Carroll's 101 problems
solo; two players can challenge each other. No math skills needed — just
careful reading. The original board game book is free online.
Did You Know?
Lewis Carroll wrote The Game of Logic in 1887, four years before the symbolic
logic now taught in universities was standardized. His counter-based system
was an independent invention of diagrammatic logic, predating Venn diagrams
by only a few years — and Carroll claimed his method was easier for
beginners.
Original Victorian Description
This ingenious game consists of a cardboard Diagram and nine Counters — four
red and five grey. A red counter signifies that a compartment is occupied by
at least one thing ('some'), while a grey counter signifies it is empty
('none'). By placing these counters on the Diagram according to logical
propositions, the player represents premises and reads off their valid
conclusion — solving syllogisms without resorting to abstract symbols. 'There
foam'd rebellious Logic, gagg'd and bound.'
Source: The Game of Logic (1887)
Heritage Parlor · Page 2 of 2
